title: Java Program Structure

introduction.what_it_is: A Java program is a collection of objects that communicate via invoking each other's methods. At its core, the Java Program Structure refers to the standard hierarchical organization of code in a Java source file, consisting of a documentation section, package declaration, import statements, interface statements, class definitions, and the main method.
introduction.why_important: Understanding the structure is essential because Java is a strictly object-oriented language. A well-organized structure ensures code readability, maintainability, and strict adherence to the compiler's requirements. Every Java program must have at least one class and a main method as the entry point.
real_world: Building large-scale enterprise applications where code needs to be modularized using packages and imported across different files.
Developing open-source libraries where documentation, clear class boundaries, and structural consistency are critical for contributors.

detailed_theory:
A typical Java program file (usually ending with a `.java` extension) is logically divided into several sections. The recommended order is:

1. **Documentation Section**: Contains comments (single-line, multi-line, or Javadoc) that describe the program, author, version, etc.
2. **Package Statement**: Defines a namespace for the class, helping in avoiding name conflicts (e.g., `package com.example.app;`). This must be the first executable line.
3. **Import Statements**: Used to bring in classes or entire packages from other namespaces (e.g., `import java.util.Scanner;`).
4. **Interface Statements**: Declarations of interfaces, which are blueprints for classes.
5. **Class Definitions**: The core building blocks of Java. A program can have multiple classes, but only one public class per file. The file name must match the public class name.
6. **Main Method Class**: The entry point of the application, defined as `public static void main(String[] args)`. The JVM starts execution from this method.

Java's structure enforces encapsulation and modularity. Everything must be inside a class, ensuring that the object-oriented paradigm is strictly followed.

features.name: Strictly Object-Oriented
features.description: Everything must be enclosed within a class or interface. No standalone methods or variables exist outside a class.

features.name: Case-Sensitive
features.description: Java is highly case-sensitive. `MyClass` and `myclass` are considered different identifiers.

features.name: Mandatory Main Method
features.description: For an application to be executable, it must contain a `main` method with a very specific signature.

features.name: Single Public Class per File
features.description: A `.java` file can contain multiple classes, but only one can be declared `public`, and its name must match the file name.

advantages: High Readability: The rigid structure makes Java code uniform and easy to read across different projects.
Modularity: Packages and import statements allow code to be neatly organized into reusable modules.
Prevents Name Clashes: The package system inherently prevents naming conflicts even in massive codebases.
limitations: Verbosity: Java requires a significant amount of boilerplate code just to write a simple program.
Rigid Rules: Strict naming conventions for files and public classes can sometimes be annoying for small scripts.

syntax.explanation: A standard Java program structure consists of optional package and import declarations followed by a mandatory class containing a main method.
breakdown: `public class MyProgram { ... }`: The primary public class definition.
`public static void main(String[] args) { ... }`: The starting point for JVM execution.
`System.out.println(...);`: Standard statement to output data to the console.
rules: The `package` statement (if present) must be the first line of code (excluding comments).
`import` statements must appear after the package statement and before any class definition.
Only one `public` class is allowed per `.java` file.
The name of the `.java` file must exactly match the name of the `public` class, including case.
Execution strictly begins at `public static void main(String[] args)`.

examples.title: Hello World Program
examples.explanation: This is the simplest Java program. It consists of a single public class `HelloWorld`. Inside it is the `main` method, which is the entry point. `System.out.println` prints text to the console.

examples.title: Program with Package and Imports
examples.explanation: This example shows the use of the `package` statement to group the class, and `import` to bring in the `Scanner` class from the `java.util` package for reading user input.

examples.title: Multiple Classes in a Single File
examples.explanation: Here we define multiple classes in one file. `MainApp` is the public class, so the file must be named `MainApp.java`. The `Helper` class has default access and is used within the `main` method.

comparisons.explanation: A file can have only one public class, which must match the filename. It can have multiple non-public classes. Public classes are accessible from other packages, while default classes are only accessible within the same package.

methods.name: main
methods.description: The core method where Java application execution begins. It must be declared as public, static, and void.
methods.parameters: String[] args - Command line arguments passed to the program.
methods.return_value: void - It does not return any value.

mistakes.title: Filename and Class Name Mismatch
mistakes.description: Naming the file something different from the public class name.
mistakes.wrong_example:
// File named MyApp.java
public class MyApplication { ... }
mistakes.correct_example:
// File named MyApplication.java
public class MyApplication { ... }
mistakes.debugging_tip: The Java compiler will throw an error: 'class MyApplication is public, should be declared in a file named MyApplication.java'.

mistakes.title: Missing 'static' in main method
mistakes.description: Declaring the main method without the 'static' keyword.
mistakes.debugging_tip: The code will compile, but running it will throw: 'Main method is not static in class ...'.

mistakes.title: Package statement not at the top
mistakes.description: Placing the package statement after imports or class declarations.
mistakes.debugging_tip: Compiler error: 'class, interface, or enum expected'.

basic.question: What is the standard structure of a Java program?
basic.answer: A Java program typically consists of (in order): Documentation, Package declaration, Import statements, Interfaces, and Classes. At least one class with a main method is required for execution.

basic.question: Can a Java file have multiple classes?
basic.answer: Yes, a Java file can contain multiple classes, but only one of them can be declared public. The filename must match the name of the public class.

intermediate.question: Why is the main method declared as `public static void main`?
intermediate.answer: `public` makes it accessible everywhere. `static` allows JVM to invoke it without instantiating the class. `void` means it doesn't return anything. `main` is the specific name JVM looks for.

intermediate.question: What happens if a Java file does not have a public class?
intermediate.answer: If there is no public class, the file can be named anything (with a `.java` extension), as long as it's a valid identifier. However, it's a best practice to match the filename with the class containing the main method.

advanced.question: How does the package structure correspond to the physical directory structure in Java?
advanced.answer: In Java, the package name dictates the folder structure where the compiled `.class` files must reside. For example, `package com.mycompany.app;` requires the compiled class to be in a directory path `com/mycompany/app/`.

advanced.question: Can we overload the main method in Java?
advanced.answer: Yes, the main method can be overloaded just like any other method. However, the JVM will only call the one with the exact signature `public static void main(String[] args)` as the entry point.

easy: Write a simple Java program that prints 'Hello Java' to the console. Save it in a file named `Hello.java`.
Create a Java program containing two classes (none public) and verify what `.class` files are generated upon compilation.
medium: Write a Java program that defines a package `org.test` and imports `java.time.LocalDate` to print the current date.
Write a program with an overloaded main method and demonstrate that only the standard main method is called by the JVM.
hard: Create a directory structure representing a package `com.bank.core`. Write a class in this package, compile it from the root directory, and run it using the fully qualified class name.

coding_challenges.statement: Write a program named `GreeterApp` that takes a command-line argument representing a name and prints 'Hello, [name]!'. If no argument is provided, print 'Hello, Guest!'.
coding_challenges.constraints: The program must handle empty command line arguments without throwing an `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`.

real_world_applications.explanation: Large Java applications use rigid package structures (e.g., `com.company.controller`, `com.company.service`, `com.company.model`) to enforce separation of concerns and maintainability.

Well-structured Java libraries use the documentation section extensively (Javadoc) and export specific packages to define a clear public API, heavily relying on Java's access modifiers and structural rules.

best_practices: Always keep the file name exactly the same as the public class name.
Group related classes into packages to avoid naming collisions and improve project structure.
Put the `main` method in a dedicated entry-point class to separate startup logic from business logic.
Use descriptive Javadoc comments at the top of every file to explain its overall purpose and author details.
summary: The Java program structure dictates the required and optional sections of a Java source file.
Order matters: Package declarations first, followed by imports, and then class declarations.
Every Java application needs an entry point: `public static void main(String[] args)`.
Strict adherence to the structure ensures modularity, encapsulation, and seamless compilation.

quiz.question: Which of the following is the correct order of elements in a Java file?
quiz.explanation: In Java, if a package is defined, it must be the first line. Imports follow the package, and finally the class declarations.

quiz.question: How many public classes can exist in a single Java file?
quiz.explanation: A Java file can have at most one public class, and if it does, the filename must match the name of that public class.

quiz.question: What is the mandatory signature of the method where execution begins?
quiz.explanation: The JVM strictly looks for the public, static, and void method named main taking an array of Strings as its argument.
